zotero.abstractNote: The Einstein Telescope (ET) will be a third-generation gravitational-wave observatory capable of detecting binary black hole mergers up to high redshifts (z 20). The large number of expected detections and the high signal-to-noise ratio of these events pose significant challenges for standard Bayesian inference methods, which are computationally expensive. We present a fast and accurate parameter estimation pipeline for ET based on neural posterior estimation (NPE). We demonstrate that our method can recover the parameters of high-redshift binary black hole mergers with high accuracy and precision, while being several orders of magnitude faster than traditional sampling methods. We apply our pipeline to a population of simulated ET sources and show that it can efficiently characterize the properties of high-redshift mergers, providing a powerful tool for future gravitational-wave cosmology and astrophysics.
